title: Chapter 21: The Revenge List
content: The door to the surface rattled open on rusted tracks. Ash moved through it without hesitation, a shadow peeling away from the dark. Wren followed three steps behind, her pulse a metronome of wrong-wrong-wrong.

They climbed a maintenance shaft that smelled of iron and stagnant water. Ash's tattoos threw faint blue light against the curved walls, serpent lines rippling with each pull of his arms. He hadn't told her where they were going. Just: *Come with me tonight.*
She'd said yes before she could think.

The shaft emptied into an alley behind a high-rise. The city roared above them—screens stacked ten stories tall, selling things to people who'd never want for anything. The air tasted like exhaust and synthetic vanilla from the perfume dispensers rigged into the streetlamps.
Ash pressed his palm flat against the building's service entrance.
The lock clicked. A glitch. His Stage 4 pushed harder than hers could, bending the digital architecture until it simply... gave.
"Watch," he said. "And don't interfere."
They took the service elevator to the forty-third floor.

The hallway was hushed, carpet thick enough to swallow footsteps. Ash moved like he knew the layout—because he did. He'd been here before. Multiple times. Wren could tell by the way his hand found the security panel without looking, the way the glitch rippled out from his fingers and the camera in the corner gave a tiny mechanical shudder before going still.
Apartment 43-B. He pressed his ear to the door.
Inside, a television murmured. A man coughed.
Ash glitched the lock and stepped inside like he owned the place.

The man on the couch was fifty-ish, silver at the temples, wearing a silk robe open over a wine-stained undershirt. Gerald Hoffman, former counsel for NeuroScale's regulatory division. The man who'd drafted the legality that allowed ANX-00 trials to continue after the first subject died.
He didn't see Ash. Didn't see Wren.
That was the cruelest part. He didn't see anything at all.
Ash walked past him to the study. Wren followed, throat tight.
The desk held three monitors, a tablet, and a half-eaten sandwich. Ash sat down and his fingers moved across the keyboard with the speed of someone who'd done this a hundred times.
"What are you doing?" Wren whispered.
"What he did to me." Ash's voice was flat. Calm. "He made me invisible. I'm returning the favor."
She watched the screen.

Bank records. Investment portfolios. Trust funds for two children attending private academies in the north district. Ash opened each account, each fund, each line of credit. He didn't drain them. He didn't steal.
He deleted them.
Every trace of Gerald Hoffman's financial existence—erased. Tax records gone. Property deeds nullified. The digital architecture of a thirty-year legal career, dismantled in twelve minutes.
Hoffman wouldn't know until morning. When his cards declined. When his phone stopped working. When the school called to say they had no record of his children's enrollment.
When he became, in the eyes of every system that mattered, a nonperson.
Ash stood. Cracked his knuckles.
"Satisfying," he said.
They left the way they came. Through the service corridor, down the elevator, into the alley. The city noise wrapped around them, indifferent.
Wren's hands were shaking. She shoved them into her pockets.
"How many?" she asked.
"List has twenty-seven names. I've done eleven."
"Twenty-seven."

"Executives. Lawyers. The two senators who buried the inquiry. Three doctors who signed off on the death certificates—my death certificate." He said it without inflection. A grocery list. "The venture capitalists who funded the next round after I was gone. A journalist who took the payoff to kill the story."
Ash stopped walking. Turned to face her under the bleeding neon of a pharmacy sign.
His tattoos pulsed faintly, blue-green in the pink light. He looked at her the way he always looked at her—hungry and guarded, both at once.
"You're quiet," he said.
"I'm processing."
"You're impressed." A ghost of a smile. "That's okay. You're allowed to be."
She was impressed. That was the problem.

The efficiency of it. The surgical precision. No violence, no screaming, no bodies to explain. Just the slow, systemic annihilation of everything a person had built. She understood the poetry of it—an eye for an eye, an erasure for an erasure.
But there was something else curling in her stomach. Something cold.
She'd watched him sit at that desk and erase a man's life with the same expression she used to wear filing expense reports. Bored. Methodical. Slightly annoyed at the tedium.
That scared her more than anything.

Because she recognized it. The detachment. The way you could turn a person into data and then delete the data and feel nothing. She'd done it herself, hadn't she? Every time she'd walked past a homeless person. Every time she'd ignored a coworker's tears in the bathroom.
The only difference was scale.
"Ash," she started.
"Don't." He kept walking. "Not tonight. Tonight was a good night."

Back in the Underlayer, the tunnels were quiet. The other invisibles slept in their alcoves, curled against the damp walls like cats. Ash walked her to the chamber she'd claimed as her own—a former pump room with a cot and a battery lantern.
He paused at the doorway.
"You'll sleep better now," he said. "Knowing something's being done."
Wren sat on the cot. The mattress smelled like mildew and old plastic.
"Ash."
He turned.
She wanted to say: *I'm terrified of you.* She wanted to say: *I'm terrified because I understand you.* She wanted to say: *What happens when the list runs out?*
Instead, she said: "Goodnight."
He nodded once and disappeared into the dark, his tattoos fading like embers.
Wren lay awake for hours.
She kept seeing Gerald Hoffman on the couch, wine-stained and oblivious. She kept seeing Ash's fingers on the keyboard, methodical and clean.
She kept seeing herself in the reflection of the monitor—a face the system couldn't quite register, flickering at the edges.
*You're becoming the same thing,* a voice whispered. Not Ash's voice. Her own.
She pulled the thin blanket over her head and tried not to think about lists.
Tried not to think about what name she might add if she had one of her own.
